lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Yet, countrymen, O, yet hold up your heads!

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: What bastard doth not? Who will go with me?

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: I will proclaim my name about the field:

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: I am the son of Marcus Cato, ho!

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: A foe to tyrants, and my country’s friend;

lines.speaker: CATO
lines.text: I am the son of Marcus Cato, ho!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And I am Brutus, Marcus Brutus, I;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Brutus, my country’s friend; know me for Brutus!

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: O young and noble Cato, art thou down?

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: Why, now thou diest as bravely as Titinius;

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: And mayst be honour’d, being Cato’s son.

lines.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
lines.text: Yield, or thou diest.

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: Only I yield to die:

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: There is so much that thou wilt kill me straight;

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: Kill Brutus, and be honour’d in his death.

lines.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
lines.text: We must not. A noble prisoner!

lines.speaker: SECOND SOLDIER
lines.text: Room, ho! Tell Antony, Brutus is ta’en.

lines.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
lines.text: I’ll tell the news. Here comes the general.

lines.speaker: FIRST SOLDIER
lines.text: Brutus is ta’en, Brutus is ta’en, my lord.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Where is he?

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: Safe, Antony; Brutus is safe enough:

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: I dare assure thee that no enemy

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: Shall ever take alive the noble Brutus:

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: The gods defend him from so great a shame!

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: When you do find him, or alive or dead,

lines.speaker: LUCILIUS
lines.text: He will be found like Brutus, like himself.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: This is not Brutus, friend; but, I assure you,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: A prize no less in worth: keep this man safe;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Give him all kindness: I had rather have

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Such men my friends than enemies. Go on,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And see whether Brutus be alive or dead;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And bring us word unto Octavius’ tent

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: How everything is chanced.

stage_directions.text: Alarum. Enter fighting, Soldiers of both armies; then Brutus, young Cato, Lucilius, and others.

glosses.definition: no true Roman; one not born of Roman stock (Cato's taunt)
young Cato, Portia's brother, Cato the Younger's son; he dies at Philippi
Cato's family slogan; the inherited republican identity

glosses.phrase: I am Brutus, Marcus Brutus, I
glosses.definition: Lucilius pretends to be Brutus, drawing fire away from the real Brutus; Plutarch records this ruse
I yield only to die (not as a captive)

glosses.phrase: The gods defend him from so great a shame
glosses.definition: may the gods keep Brutus from the shame of being taken alive — Lucilius's plea that Brutus has died freely

glosses.phrase: A prize no less in worth
glosses.definition: Antony recognises Lucilius's loyalty as itself valuable — an act of mercy that characterises him

annotations.title: Lucilius pretends to be Brutus — and Antony spares him
annotations.body:
The scene is short and does specific work. Lucilius, captured, announces himself as Brutus to draw fire away from the real Brutus; Antony realises the truth and, notably, spares him: 'A prize no less in worth: keep this man safe; / Give him all kindness: I had rather have / Such men my friends than enemies.'

Plutarch records the incident (*Life of Brutus* §50). Shakespeare uses it for one last characterisation of Antony: the political actor who proscribed senators in 4.1 is also, at the specific moment of victory, capable of an act of mercy. Shakespeare's Antony is not a monster; he is a calculating politician with real feeling for personal loyalty. The moment also quietly rehabilitates the political category of the honourable enemy — an idea the play keeps alive even as its main action shows its disappearance. Lucilius, who spoke the scene's most moving lines ('The gods defend him from so great a shame'), has acted as Brutus's surrogate; Antony treats him as one.

trials.prompt: Why does Lucilius pretend to be Brutus?
options: To draw the enemy's fire and let the real Brutus escape — a historical incident Shakespeare takes from Plutarch; Antony recognises the trick and, notably, spares Lucilius
